brief: GAP/RATIONALE: EPS and catheter ablation guidelines for Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) are age-based, but body size/weight may be a more relevant determinant of ablation outcomes; weight-outcome relationship in pediatric WPW unclear. DESIGN/SETTING: multicenter retrospective cohort study using the IMPACT (Improving Pediatric and Adult Congenital Treatment) registry (NCDR); enrollment period April 2016 to December 2019; 84 centers. POPULATION: children aged 1-21 years with WPW undergoing first-time EPS; exclusions = congenital heart disease, cardiomyopathy, and >1 ablation target. Total n=4,456 subjects. COMPARISON/EXPOSURE: weight threshold of 30 kg (=1 SD below cohort mean); cohort split into <30 kg vs >=30 kg. 14% of subjects weighed <30 kg. OUTCOMES: primary = major adverse events (MAEs); additional = deferred ablation, use of cryoablation, ablation success. RESULTS (numbers): <30 kg vs >=30 kg group differences -- preprocedural SVT more common in <30 kg (45% vs 29%; P<0.001); right septal accessory pathways less common in <30 kg (25% vs 33%; P<0.001); MAEs rare but higher in <30 kg (0.3% vs 0.05%; P=0.04); deferred ablation no difference (9% vs 12%; P=0.07); cryoablation use no difference (11% vs 11%; P=0.70); ablation success higher in <30 kg (95% vs 92%; P=0.009); success effect persisted after covariate adjustment (odds ratio 1.6; 95% CI 1.01-2.70; P=0.046). TAKEAWAY: weight <30 kg associated with a small but elevated risk of MAEs; deferred ablation and cryoablation rates similar across weight groups; after adjusting for factors including accessory pathway type and location, weight <30 kg was an independent predictor of acute ablation success.
